catalog.title: Writing · Tone down drama; tone up clarity
catalog.description: Tone down drama; tone up clarity · A2 writing in “Culture & integration context”: Dutch you can use the same week, guided steps, and a short check at the end.

pedagogy.objective: By the end of this lesson, you can write a short practical message about culture & integration context using a model, gaps, and a final checklist
can_do_outcomes: Understand short texts about traditions without exam-specific copying
Name major Dutch holidays and what people typically do
Write a short practical message with a clear request.
pedagogy.grammar_primary_label: Fixed phrases for traditions and comparison

examples_nl: Mag ik vragen hoe jullie Koningsdag vieren?
In Nederland dragen sommige mensen oranje.
examples_en: May I ask how you celebrate King's Day?
In the Netherlands some people wear orange.

examples_nl: Je spreekt hier vaak rustig aan bij de balie.
Men plant sociale afspraken vaak een dag van tevoren.
examples_en: Here people often address the desk calmly.
People often plan social meetups a day ahead.

grammar_points.point: fixed expressions for holidays and greetings
examples_nl: Fijne feestdagen en tot volgend jaar!
Integratie is taal én gewoontes leren.
examples_en: Happy holidays and see you next year!
Integration is language and learning habits.

micro_outcomes: I can recognise key words about culture & integration context in short Dutch texts or audio.
I can copy at least one useful chunk into a sentence about myself.
I can finish most guided tasks in the lesson without peeking at translations on the second try.
I can pass the quick self-check at the end of the lesson.
I know what to revise tomorrow in two minutes or less.

steps.activity:
Welcome! This lesson is built in small steps so you always know where you are.

**What you'll be able to do:** Write a short practical message about culture & integration context using a model, gaps, and a final checklist

**Grammar thread today:** Fixed phrases for traditions and comparison — you'll get explanations, examples, and production tasks in **Language focus** and **Guided practice**.

Take your time — you can repeat any step. Try to say at least one Dutch sentence out loud today.
steps.teacher_notes: Keep under ~1 min; reassure anxious learners.

steps.activity:
Let's wake up vocabulary about **Culture & integration context**.

Words you might use today:
• **Koningsdag**
• **Sinterklaas**
• **kerst**
• **oud en nieuw**
• **basisschool**
• **ouderspreekuur**
• **directheid**
• **fietsen**

**Your turn (1–2 short sentences, Dutch or mixed):**
**Wat deed je gisteren?** — *What did you do yesterday?*

If you only know a few words, that's fine — start there.
steps.example_response: Gisteren sprak ik met een vriend over **Koningsdag** en Nederlandse tradities. We dronken koffie en wandelden een half uur door de buurt.

steps.activity:
**Model message (Dutch)**

Subject: Vraag over bon

Goedemiddag,

Gisteren heb ik boodschappen gedaan bij uw winkel. Ik mis de bon in mijn tas. Kunt u een kopie mailen?

Met vriendelijke groet,
[jouw naam]

**Notice:** polite opening, short problem, clear request, closing.

**Later you will write:** Schrijf 6 zinnen over een Nederlandse traditie die je interessant vindt en waarom.

**Today's grammar — culture chunks**

**Rule:** Fixed phrases beat abstract essays: *Mag ik vragen …?* · *Bij ons …* · *Hoe vieren jullie …?*

**Patterns:**
• *Eerlijk gezegd …* · *Ik wil geen gedoe maken, maar …*

**Produce:**
1. Ask one respectful question about a Dutch tradition.
2. Compare with *Bij ons is dat anders, want …* (short).

**Pronunciation (listen + repeat)**
**Pronunciation — final -n:** *eten, gaan* — don’t swallow the *-n* completely in careful speech.

**Language focus — patterns you can reuse**

**impersonal constructions (men …, je … as 'one')**
• NL: Mag ik vragen hoe jullie Koningsdag vieren? / In Nederland dragen sommige mensen oranje.
• EN: May I ask how you celebrate King's Day? / In the Netherlands some people wear orange.

**present tense narratives about customs**
• NL: Je spreekt hier vaak rustig aan bij de balie. / Men plant sociale afspraken vaak een dag van tevoren.
• EN: Here people often address the desk calmly. / People often plan social meetups a day ahead.

**fixed expressions for holidays and greetings**
• NL: Fijne feestdagen en tot volgend jaar! / Integratie is taal én gewoontes leren.
• EN: Happy holidays and see you next year! / Integration is language and learning habits.

**Try this:** pick one Dutch sentence above and change only the time or the person (ik → wij / vandaag → morgen), **or** do the “Produce” task from the grammar box above.


**Culture tip:** This unit foregrounds situated cultural knowledge; every lesson links language to real Netherlands contexts.
*How does this compare with where you live? One sentence in your own words.*

**1 — Label the parts:** opening / problem / request / closing in the model email.

**2 — Gap-fill:** Met vriendelijke ______, [naam].

**3 — Politeness:** choose softer Dutch: **Ik wil** → **Ik zou graag willen** in one sentence.

**4 — Combine:** Link with **want**: *Ik bel morgen, want ik ben vandaag druk.*

**5 — Shorten:** remove three words without losing the message.

**6 — Checklist tick:** subject line clear? closing present?

**7 — Your details:** replace [naam] and add one real detail from your life.

**8 — Read aloud:** read your draft slowly.
---

**All four skills — finish this lesson with these four micro-tasks**

🎧 **Listening:** Use TTS on this Dutch line (slow, then closer to natural): *Mag ik vragen hoe jullie Koningsdag vieren?*

📖 **Reading:** Read silently, then say the **main idea in one English sentence**: *Mag ik vragen hoe jullie Koningsdag vieren?*

✍️ **Writing:** Write **one new Dutch sentence** that uses **Koningsdag** and fits *Fixed phrases for traditions and comparison*.

🗣️ **Speaking:** Read your new sentence **aloud twice** — exaggerate stress the first time, normal the second.

*(If this lesson was already heavy on one skill, treat that skill as “extra reps” — the point is you always touch all four.)*
steps.teacher_notes: Mix modalities; at least 6 tasks — all concrete above.

steps.activity:
**Writing task**

Schrijf 6 zinnen over een Nederlandse traditie die je interessant vindt en waarom.

**Phrase bank:**
• Koningsdag
• Sinterklaas
• kerst
• oud en nieuw
• basisschool
• ouderspreekuur
• directheid
• fietsen
• agenda
• borrel
• traditie
• integratie

**Checklist before you finish:**
• Clear subject or first line
• Polite greeting
• Short paragraphs
• **Met vriendelijke groet** + name

**Quick self-check**

Try each question, then tap **Check answer**. The correct answer stays hidden until you submit.

**Tip:** Say Dutch options out loud if that helps.

**Afterwards:** If three or more felt easy, open **Quiz** for a short recap. If not, go back to **Guided practice** once more.
steps.teacher_notes: Rendered with interactive UI when interaction is present.

items.prompt: Pick the best reply when someone says **Hoe gaat het?**
items.feedbackCorrect: Nice — this is a natural, friendly reply.
items.feedbackIncorrect: **Tot ziens** is goodbye; **Graag gedaan** fits after *dank je*. After *Hoe gaat het?* people often say how they feel, e.g. **Goed, dank je!**

items.hint: Present tense of *leren* (to learn), 1st person singular.

items.prompt: Which opening sounds **more formal** when you talk to a stranger or in a shop?
items.feedbackCorrect: Right — **u** + verb form (*Kunt u…*) is the usual polite pattern.
items.feedbackIncorrect: **Kunt u** with *u* is more formal; **Kun je** is fine with friends.

items.prompt: In Dutch, yes/no questions often put the **verb before the subject** (e.g. *Ga je mee?*).
items.feedbackCorrect: Correct — verb-first order is typical for yes/no questions.
items.feedbackIncorrect: This is **true**: many yes/no questions start with the verb.

items.prompt: Say or write **one new sentence** using **Koningsdag**.
items.exampleAnswer: Example: *Ik wil graag Koningsdag met mijn nieuwe buren.* (adjust to your situation.)

steps.activity:
**Nice work.**

Next in this unit: **Real-life task · When manners matter as much as grammar** — we keep building **Culture & integration context** with a new focus.

**2-minute revision:** skim the phrase bank from step 6 once more.

When you're ready, open **Quiz** for a compact recap — or **Flashcards** for the lemmas.

**Dutch culture insight**

**Koningsdag** orange, **Sinterklaas** debates, and **kerst** traditions differ by family; curiosity beats assumptions. *Hoe vieren jullie …?* is a respectful opener.

**Phrases — school & integration tone**

| Dutch | English |
|-------|--------|
| *Mag ik een afspraak met de leerkracht?* | May I have an appointment with the teacher? |
| *Mijn kind heeft moeite met huiswerk.* | My child struggles with homework. |
| *Hoe meld ik mijn kind ziek?* | How do I report my child sick? |

**Mini dialogue**

**Ouder:** Goedemiddag, ik bel over de ouderavond volgende week.
**School:** Zeker. De avond begint om 19:30; u krijgt een link per e-mail.

**Say or write:** Ask one respectful question about a Dutch tradition with *Mag ik vragen …?*

*Optional reflection:* In one English sentence, what feels similar or different where you live?
steps.teacher_notes: Link to next lesson id in CMS when wired.

quiz_ideas: Self-check: choose the best reply to "Hoe gaat het?" (see step 7 in the lesson).
Gap-fill idea: write a sentence with **Koningsdag** in the present tense.
True/false: in Dutch yes/no questions the verb often comes before the subject.
Say one new polite request starting with **Kunt u …** or **Mag ik …**.
Paraphrase one Dutch line from the lesson input in your own English.
assessment.success_criteria: You can complete the step-7 self-check comfortably and produce at least one clear Dutch sentence about the lesson theme (spoken or written).

content_outline.writing_prompts_nl: Schrijf 6 zinnen over een Nederlandse traditie die je interessant vindt en waarom.
